symbols: young Samson overpowering a lion — supernatural strength from the Spirit of God
bare hands — no weapon needed when God empowers
lion — the fierce predator overcome by divinely granted power
youthful body — the surprising vessel through which God's strength manifests
wild landscape — the vineyards of Timnah where the encounter occurred
description: A dramatic painting showing a muscular young man wrestling and tearing open the jaws of a large lion. The youth, wearing only a headband and no clothing, straddles the fallen lion and forces its mouth open with both hands. His expression is one of intense effort and determination. The lion lies on its back, claws extended, in its death throes. The setting is a wild, rocky landscape with vegetation. The painting has an academic realist style with careful attention to anatomy and the dramatic tension of the struggle.
theological_description: The young Samson's slaying of the lion is recorded in Judges 14:5-6: 'Then Samson went down with his father and mother to Timnah, and they came to the vineyards of Timnah. And behold, a young lion came toward him roaring. Then the Spirit of the Lord rushed upon him, and although he had nothing in his hand, he tore the lion in pieces as one would tear a young goat.' The key theological detail is that Samson's strength was not natural but charismatic — it came from 'the Spirit of the Lord rushing upon him.' This is the first manifestation of Samson's supernatural empowerment, establishing the pattern that his strength was God's gift, not his own achievement. The episode also becomes the basis for Samson's riddle at his wedding feast: 'Out of the eater came something to eat, out of the strong came something sweet' (Judges 14:14), as honey later formed in the lion's carcass. In Christian typology, Samson's victory over the lion prefigures Christ's victory over the 'roaring lion' Satan (1 Peter 5:8). David also slew a lion while protecting his flock (1 Samuel 17:34-36), establishing lion-slaying as a sign of the God-empowered deliverer.

Samson's riddle from the lion — out of the eater came something to eat

David's lion-slaying as a parallel act of God-empowered deliverance
